Title: Strigolactone signaling regulates specialized metabolism in tobacco stems and interactions with stem-feeding herbivores

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: F-box protein, strigolactone/karrikin signaling coreceptor component
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: stem epidermis for anthocyanins; root-shoot junction/stem pith for nicotine and phenolamides
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Silencing MAX2 increases stem anthocyanins and phenolamides, decreases nicotine, and increases susceptibility to the stem-boring weevil.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. RNAi silencing of NaMAX2a/b caused increased branching and red stems with ~2-fold higher anthocyanin accumulation. 2. max2 plants showed elevated JA, JA-Ile and IAA, increased CP and DCS, but reduced nicotine in root-shoot junction tissues. 3. max2 plants were more susceptible to Trichobaris mucorea larvae, linking MAX2-dependent SL signaling to defense metabolite balance.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: stable RNAi silencing; metabolite profiling; herbivore bioassays; hormone quantification; microarray; subcellular localization; Y2H
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Measured in axillary buds for silencing assays; functional effects characterized in stems, root-shoot junctions and roots
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Trichobaris mucorea infestation; low phosphate for SL exudate measurements; GR24 and KAR treatments
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: MAX2 mediates both SL and KAR signaling generally, but the metabolic and defense phenotypes in this paper were specific to SL signaling because kai2 plants lacked these effects.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Maintaining or enhancing MAX2-dependent SL signaling may help preserve nicotine-based stem defense and reduce susceptibility to stem-boring herbivores, though it may reduce branching.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced SL signaling increases branching but compromises stem defense through reduced nicotine despite elevated JA-associated metabolites.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Microarray, accession NA; untargeted LC-qTOF metabolomics, accession NA
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Anthocyanins and phenolamides are defense/stress-associated specialized metabolites; nicotine is a major anti-herbivore toxin slowing larval growth.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: stem epidermis for anthocyanins; root-shoot junction/stem pith for nicotine and phenolamides
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Silencing D14 increases anthocyanins and phenolamides, lowers nicotine, and increases susceptibility to stem-boring larvae.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. d14 RNAi plants phenocopied max2 with increased branching and red stems. 2. d14 plants accumulated more anthocyanin, CP and DCS and had lower nicotine than control plants. 3. d14 plants supported greater T. mucorea larval growth, showing that SL perception is required for proper defense metabolite homeostasis.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: stable RNAi silencing; metabolite profiling; herbivore bioassays; SL quantification; protein interaction assays
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Measured in axillary buds for silencing; functional assays in stems, pith and roots
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: T. mucorea infestation; low phosphate; rac-GR24 in interaction assays
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Crosses with aoc showed JA acts downstream of D14 to regulate anthocyanin and phenolamide accumulation, while auxin additionally contributes to anthocyanin and nicotine outcomes.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: D14 is a leverage point for tuning architecture-defense tradeoffs, but downregulation risks reduced nicotine-mediated resistance.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of D14 increases branching and some JA-associated metabolites but decreases nicotine and increases herbivore susceptibility.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Nicotine is a major anti-herbivore defense; anthocyanins/phenolamides are stress and defense-associated metabolites.

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Suppressor of MAX2-like proteins, SL signaling repressors
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Accumulated SMXL6/7 enhance JA signaling output, promoting anthocyanin and phenolamide accumulation.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NaSMXL6 and NaSMXL7 are nuclear SL repressors that interact with NaD14 in the presence of GR24. 2. SMXL6/7 interact with several JAZ proteins, especially JAZb, and their accumulation in SL-RNAi plants promotes JAZb degradation. 3. SMXL6/7 interfere with JAZb-MYC2 interaction, thereby releasing JA signaling and increasing anthocyanin and phenolamide production.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: phylogeny; subcellular localization; Y2H; pull-down; co-IP; immunoblot; in vitro degradation assay; Y3H
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: SMXL7 is stem-abundant based on RNA-seq coexpression analysis
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: D14; JAZa; JAZb; JAZd; JAZj; JAZl; JAZe; MYC2a; MYC2b
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The study mechanistically places SMXL6/7 upstream of amplified JA signaling in SL-deficient plants; direct metabolite effects are mediated through JAZ/MYC2 and downstream MYB8.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: SMXL6/7 are candidate nodes for rewiring SL-JA crosstalk to tune specialized metabolism, but overaccumulation may reduce nicotine-mediated defense through broader hormonal imbalance.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Enhanced SMXL accumulation amplifies JA outputs but is associated with reduced nicotine and greater herbivore susceptibility in SL-deficient backgrounds.
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: 35S for transient expression in localization/interaction assays
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq tissue coexpression dataset from published source; accession not provided in text
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Anthocyanins and phenolamides are defense/stress-associated metabolites.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidatively cleaves 9-cis-β-carotene to generate the apocarotenoid precursor for strigolactone biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key biosynthetic step, not established as sole rate-limiting step in this paper
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: root exudates for (±)2'-epi-orobanchol; stem epidermis for anthocyanins; root-shoot junction/stem pith for nicotine and phenolamides
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Silencing CCD7 abolishes/strongly reduces strigolactone production, increases anthocyanins and phenolamides, decreases nicotine, and increases susceptibility to stem-boring larvae.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ccd7 RNAi plants showed increased branching and nearly undetectable root-exuded (±)2'-epi-orobanchol. 2. ccd7 plants accumulated more stem anthocyanin and pith phenolamides but less nicotine. 3. ccd7 plants were more susceptible to T. mucorea, showing that SL biosynthesis positively supports nicotine-based defense.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: stable RNAi silencing; targeted SL quantification from root exudates; metabolite profiling; herbivore bioassays
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Measured in seedlings for silencing; functional analyses in roots, stems and root-shoot junction
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Low phosphate for root exudate SL measurements; T. mucorea infestation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: ccd7 plants phenocopied d14 and max2 for defense-metabolic outputs, supporting a specific role of SL rather than KAR signaling.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Avoid reducing CCD7 if nicotine-mediated stem defense is desired; manipulation may alter branching and SL-dependent ecological interactions.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Silencing CCD7 increases branching but decreases herbivore resistance and nicotine.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Targeted UHPLC-MS for strigolactone; untargeted metabolomics, accession NA
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: (±)2'-epi-orobanchol is the active SL in N. attenuata; nicotine slows T. mucorea larval growth; anthocyanins/phenolamides are defense-associated.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Allene oxide cyclase, jasmonate biosynthetic enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key biosynthetic enzyme in JA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: stem epidermis for anthocyanins; root-shoot junction/stem pith for phenolamides and nicotine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Silencing AOC lowers JA signaling, abolishes induced phenolamide and anthocyanin accumulation, and increases larval performance.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. aoc plants lacked attack-induced red stem pigmentation and supported higher T. mucorea growth. 2. aoc plants had low MYB8 and DH29 transcript levels and lacked CP and DCS induction after attack. 3. Genetic crosses showed JA signaling acts downstream of SL signaling to drive anthocyanin and phenolamide accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: previously characterized RNAi line; herbivore bioassays; hormone and metabolite profiling; transcript analysis; genetic crosses
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: T. mucorea infestation; IAA treatment; decapitation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: AOC is used as a causal pathway benchmark to establish JA dependence of SL-regulated anthocyanin and phenolamide sectors; auxin effects on nicotine remain at least partially JA-independent.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Maintaining JA biosynthesis is important for phenolamide and anthocyanin induction, but alone does not guarantee nicotine-mediated stem defense when auxin is elevated.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced JA biosynthesis increases herbivore susceptibility and collapses phenolamide accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Phenolamides and anthocyanins are defense/stress-associated metabolites; nicotine is anti-herbivore.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: root-shoot junction/stem pith; synthesized in roots and transported to shoots
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Silencing PMT reduces nicotine and increases T. mucorea larval growth.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. pmt plants had strongly reduced nicotine in pith. 2. T. mucorea larvae gained more biomass on pmt plants than on control plants. 3. These results support nicotine as the major defense metabolite underlying the susceptibility of SL-RNAi plants.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: previously characterized RNAi line; nicotine quantification; herbivore bioassays
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PMT was used as a functional comparator rather than the central focus of molecular dissection, but it directly validates the nicotine-defense relationship in this study.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Enhancing PMT or preserving its expression is a rational strategy to strengthen nicotine-mediated stem defense in Nicotiana.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Nicotine is a neurotoxic anti-herbivore alkaloid that slows T. mucorea larval growth.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers hydroxycinnamoyl moieties onto polyamine acceptors to form defensive phenolamides.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Upregulated DH29 expression is associated with increased phenolamide accumulation in SL-RNAi plants in a JA-dependent manner.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DH29 transcripts were reduced in JA-deficient aoc pith and elevated in max2, d14 and ccd7 pith. 2. CP and DCS induction after herbivore attack required JA biosynthesis and correlated with DH29 expression. 3. DH29 acts downstream of SL-JA crosstalk to promote phenolamide accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-qPCR; metabolite profiling; comparison across RNAi genotypes and genetic crosses
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in root-shoot junction/stem pith, especially after herbivory and in SL-RNAi lines
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Despite strong effects on phenolamides, this pathway branch did not measurably alter T. mucorea performance in myb8 plants.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: DH29 may be useful for engineering phenolamide accumulation, but this may not improve resistance to stem-boring weevils in this system.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Enhanced phenolamide production did not translate into improved resistance against T. mucorea.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Phenolamides contribute to defense against some herbivores, though not against T. mucorea in this stem system.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: BBL, berberine bridge enzyme-like oxidase; A622, isoflavone reductase-like protein
Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: Berberine bridge enzyme-like protein; Isoflavone reductase-like protein A622
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: roots for biosynthesis; root-shoot junction/stem pith for accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Transcript levels of BBL and A622 are suppressed by auxin in both EV and aoc/max2 backgrounds, consistent with reduced nicotine accumulation when auxin is high.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BBL and A622 transcripts were lower in max2 seedlings than in EV seedlings. 2. Exogenous IAA reduced BBL and A622 transcript levels in both EV and aoc seedlings, indicating auxin-mediated repression independent of JA. 3. Their repression is associated with reduced nicotine accumulation in SL-RNAi plants.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-qPCR under IAA treatment; genotype comparisons; nicotine quantification under decapitation/CFM treatments
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Measured in seedlings; nicotine synthesized in roots
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 10 μM IAA treatment for 8 h; decapitation; CFM treatment; T. mucorea infestation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: These genes were used as readouts of nicotine biosynthetic capacity rather than direct transgenic targets in this paper.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Reducing auxin repression or strengthening BBL/A622 expression may help maintain nicotine under altered hormonal states.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Nicotine is a major anti-herbivore alkaloid.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: root-shoot junction/stem pith for phenolamides; stem epidermis for anthocyanin
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: MYB8 positively regulates phenolamide accumulation downstream of JA and SL-JA crosstalk, but these phenolamides do not affect T. mucorea performance.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. myb8 plants accumulated much lower CP and DCS than EV plants. 2. T. mucorea larval growth was unchanged on myb8 plants, indicating phenolamides are not major defenses in stem pith against this herbivore. 3. MYB8 transcript abundance was reduced in aoc plants and elevated in max2/d14/ccd7 plants, placing it downstream of JA and SL signaling.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Key activator of phenolamide biosynthesis in N. attenuata stems/pith
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates DH29 and promotes CP/DCS accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: previously characterized RNAi line; metabolite profiling; herbivore bioassays; transcript analysis
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in root-shoot junction/stem pith; induced under JA-competent conditions and elevated in SL-RNAi plants
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although anthocyanin is discussed as partly MYB8-mediated in the model, direct target validation in this paper focused on phenolamides.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: MYB8 is suitable for engineering phenolamides, but this may not enhance resistance to stem-boring weevils.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Redirecting flux to phenolamides may not benefit all herbivore defense contexts.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Phenolamides are defense-associated metabolites, especially against some chewing herbivores.

Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: JAZb is destabilized in SL-deficient/insensitive backgrounds through interaction with SMXL6/7, thereby releasing JA signaling to increase anthocyanin and phenolamide accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. JAZb physically interacts with SMXL6/7 in vivo and in yeast-supported assays. 2. Recombinant JAZb degraded faster in crude extracts from max2 and d14 pith than in EV or kai2 extracts. 3. Increasing SMXL6/7 abundance weakened JAZb-MYC2a/b interaction, mechanistically linking SL deficiency to amplified JA outputs.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: SL signaling status via SMXL6/7 accumulation; JA signaling pathway
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller connecting SL repressors to JA-regulated anthocyanin and phenolamide accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Represses MYC2-dependent downstream activation of MYB8/DH29 and associated metabolic genes when stable
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Acts in JAZ-MYC2 repressor complex; interacts with SMXL6/7
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Y2H; Co-IP; in vitro degradation assay; Y3H
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: JAZb highly coexpressed with stem-abundant SMXL7 based on RNA-seq tissue atlas
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Methionine titration in Y3H; SL-deficient genetic backgrounds
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: JAZb was selected from several JAZs because of strong coexpression with SMXL7 and physical interaction support.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating JAZb-SMXL interactions could tune JA-responsive metabolism, but may produce pleiotropic defense/growth effects.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Destabilizing JAZb boosts some JA responses but does not restore nicotine-based resistance under high auxin.
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: 35S for transient expression; pBridge Met-repressible system in Y3H
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq tissue coexpression dataset from published source; accession not provided
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Anthocyanins and phenolamides are defense/stress-related metabolites.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: stems/pith and roots depending on metabolite
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: MYC2a/b are the released JA-responsive activators inferred to drive downstream metabolic gene activation when JAZb repression is weakened by SMXL6/7.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Y3H assays showed that increasing SMXL6/7 abundance weakened or abolished JAZb-MYC2a/b interaction. 2. The model proposes that released MYC2 activates downstream JA signaling, including MYB8, to elevate anthocyanins and phenolamides. 3. MYC2 is therefore a mechanistic effector of SL-JA crosstalk in this study.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: MYB8 and JA-responsive downstream metabolic genes
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: transcriptional activation when released from JAZ repression
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: JAZ repressors; SL status via SMXL-JAZ interaction; JA signaling
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Core JA-output activator inferred to connect JAZ destabilization to metabolite accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates downstream JA-responsive genes involved in phenolamide and likely anthocyanin/nicotine regulation
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: JAZb; SMXL6; SMXL7 (indirect competitive interference context)
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Y3H interaction interference assay; pathway inference from genetic and metabolite data
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: MYC2a/b were not themselves genetically manipulated here; their role is supported mechanistically through protein interaction interference and known JA signaling logic.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: MYC2-centered engineering could enhance specific JA outputs, but balancing nicotine, phenolamides and growth remains critical.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad JA activation can be pleiotropic and may not improve defense if auxin simultaneously suppresses nicotine.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: These metabolites contribute to defense/stress responses, with nicotine specifically impacting herbivore performance.
